disease.name: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency
disease.note: Autosomal co-dominant genetic disorder caused by SERPINA1 mutations, leading to deficient alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT). Lung disease (emphysema) results from low AAT levels. Liver disease results from toxic accumulation of misfolded AAT (PiZ) protein in hepatocytes. Expression ranges from neonatal cholestasis to adult cirrhosis and HCC.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Decompensated cirrhosis -> EMERGENCY transplant; Compensated cirrhosis -> URGENT management/Rx; Mild liver disease -> OUTPATIENT monitoring

historyFeatures.note: Neonatal cholestatic jaundice in 10-20% of PiZZ infants
Lung emphysema: usually develops in 30s-40s; panacinar, basilar predominant

bedside.name: Pulse oximetry and ABG
bedside.indication: assess for hypoxia; emphysema-related respiratory impairment

laboratory.name: Serum alpha-1 antitrypsin level (immunoturbidimetry)
laboratory.indication: screening test: <100mg/dL suggests deficiency; <50mg/dL typical for PiZZ (normal >100-200)

laboratory.name: SERPINA1 genotyping or phenotyping (isoelectric focusing)
laboratory.indication: defines Pi type: PiMM (normal), PiMZ (carrier), PiZZ (most severe), PiSZ, PiNull (very rare)

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: elevated transaminases in children; cholestatic pattern may be seen in neonatal form

laboratory.name: FBC with platelets
laboratory.indication: thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism in portal hypertension

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: liver morphology, cirrhosis assessment, HCC surveillance

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.indication: hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, basilar predominant emphysema

imaging.name: High-resolution CT chest
imaging.indication: panacinar emphysema with basilar predominance; bronchiectasis

imaging.name: Pulmonary function tests (PFTs)
imaging.indication: obstructive pattern with reduced DLCO, air trapping, hyperinflation

imaging.name: Liver biopsy
imaging.indication: characteristic: PAS-positive, diastase-resistant globules in periportal hepatocytes (AAT inclusions); fibrosis grading. May be indicated if diagnosis unclear vs other liver diseases.

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency; ERS/ATS Guidelines: AAT Deficiency

pathways.criteria: Neonate/infant with prolonged jaundice, conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, elevated LFTs, PiZZ genotype
pathways.treatment: Supportive care: ursodeoxycholic acid 15-20mg/kg/day (improves bile flow). Nutritional optimization: MCT oil-based formula, fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (ADEK). Monitor LFTs, growth, INR. Most children outgrow liver disease by age 1-2; if progressive, transplant evaluation. 10-20% develop decompensated cirrhosis requiring transplant in first decade.

pathways.criteria: Child/adolescent with elevated LFTs, hepatomegaly, PiZZ, +/- fibrosis on imaging/biopsy
pathways.treatment: HCC surveillance US + AFP q6m if advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis. Avoid alcohol (lifelong recommendation). Vaccinate for HAV, HBV. Monitor for growth, nutrition. Lung surveillance: avoid smoking (active + passive). Consider family screening of siblings. No augmentation therapy recommended for liver disease (does not address intrahepatic aggregates).

pathways.criteria: PiZZ, age >18, liver stiffness elevated, compensated cirrhosis or F3 fibrosis
pathways.treatment: HCC surveillance US + AFP q6m. Variceal screening EGD if cirrhosis. Avoid alcohol completely. Optimize metabolic health (avoid obesity/NASH). No specific pharmacotherapy for liver disease in AAT deficiency (no approved therapy targeting hepatic aggregates). Emerging therapies: RNAi-based (inclisiran, fazirsiran) in clinical trials to reduce intrahepatic Z-AAT. Lung augmentation therapy (IV Prolastin-C 60mg/kg weekly) is indicated for PiZZ patients with emphysema, but does not affect liver disease.

pathways.criteria: Child-Pugh B/C, ascites, variceal bleeding, encephalopathy, HCC
pathways.treatment: Liver transplant evaluation. Excellent outcomes post-transplant (SERCA1 phenotype becomes donor type, normal AAT levels, no disease recurrence). Manage decompensation (diuretics, lactulose, variceal banding). Combined liver-lung transplant may be considered if both advanced liver and lung disease. Manage lung disease concurrently (bronchodilators, pulmonary rehab, oxygen).

pathways.criteria: PiZZ, FEV1 30-65% predicted, obstructive lung disease with DLCO reduction
pathways.treatment: Augmentation therapy: IV human AAT (Prolastin-C, Zemaira, Glassia) 60mg/kg once weekly. Slows lung function decline. Also: standard COPD management (bronchodilators, inhaled corticosteroids, pulmonary rehab, smoking cessation, pneumococcal/influenza vaccination, oxygen if hypoxic). Lung volume reduction surgery or endobronchial valves for selected patients. Lung transplant evaluation if progressive disease.

disease.emergencyPriority: low for mild liver disease; medium for compensated cirrhosis; high for decompensated
